'''Craig Fitzgibbon''' (born 16 June 1977) is an Australian professional [[rugby league]] coach who is the head coach of [[Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks]] in the [[National Rugby League|NRL]] and a former professional [[rugby league footballer]] who played in the 1990s, 2000s and 2010s.

A [[New South Wales rugby league team|New South Wales]] [[State of Origin series|State of Origin]] and [[Australia national rugby league team|Australia international]] representative goal-kicking forward, he played in the [[National Rugby League|NRL]] for the [[Illawarra Steelers]] and [[St. George Illawarra Dragons]] as well as for the [[Sydney Roosters]], with whom he won the [[2002 NRL Premiership]]. Fitzgibbon then played in the [[Super League]] for [[Hull F.C.|Hull FC]]. He was also the last coach of the [[Country New South Wales rugby league team]].

==Early years==
The son of former professional [[rugby league]] footballer and coach [[Allan Fitzgibbon]], Craig Fitzgibbon played his first game of junior football with the Dapto under-9Bs. His heroes as a child were [[Gavin Miller]], [[Bradley Clyde]] and [[Andrew Ettingshausen]].<ref>{{cite magazine | author = Matt Logue| title = My first...|magazine=[[Rugby League Week]]|date=21 May 2008|page=33}}</ref>

==Playing career==
===National Rugby League===
Fitzgibbon began his playing career for the [[Illawarra Steelers]] in 1998. In his first season, he was the Steelers' top point-scorer and was named rookie of the year. Following the Steelers' joint-venture with [[St. George Dragons|St. George]], he played for the joint-venture club [[St. George Illawarra Dragons]]. Fitzgibbon played from the bench in the [[1999 NRL Grand Final]], scoring the first try in St. George Illawarra's 20–18 loss.{{citation needed|date=October 2022}}
[[File:Roosters Fitzy.jpg|thumb|left|Roosters forward Craig Fitzgibbon kicking a conversion in a finals match against the Warriors, 2008.]]

Fitzgibbon subsequently transferred to the [[Sydney Roosters]] in 2000. He played at second-row forward and scored a try in the Roosters' [[2000 NRL Grand Final]] loss to the [[Brisbane Broncos]]. Eventually, Fitzgibbon won a premiership with the Roosters, playing at second-row forward in their [[2002 NRL grand final]] victory over the [[New Zealand Warriors]] and winning the [[Clive Churchill Medal]] for his best-on-ground performance.{{citation needed|date=October 2022}}

Having won the [[2002 NRL Premiership]], the [[Sydney Roosters]] travelled to England to play the [[2003 World Club Challenge]] against [[Super League]] champions, [[St Helens R.F.C.]] Fitzgibbon played at second-row forward, scoring a [[Try (rugby)|try]] and kicking nine goals in Sydney's victory. In the [[2003 NRL grand final]] Fitzgibbon played in the second row and was the Roosters' goal-kicker in their loss to the [[Penrith Panthers]]. After that he was selected to go on the [[2003 Kangaroo tour]]. Fitzgibbon played for the Roosters at second-row forward in their [[2004 NRL grand final]] loss to cross-Sydney rivals, [[Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs|Canterbury-Bankstown]]. Fitzgibbon was selected in the Australian team to go and compete in the end of season [[2004 Rugby League Tri-Nations]] tournament. In the final against [[Great Britain national rugby league team|Great Britain]] he played from the interchange bench and kicked two goals in the Kangaroos' 44–4 victory.

In 2006, Fitzgibbon succeeded [[Luke Ricketson]] as captain of the [[Sydney Roosters]]. On 12 August 2006, he became the highest scoring forward in premiership history, surpassing ex-[[Canberra Raiders]] back-rower [[David Furner]]. Fitzgibbon passed the previous record of 1218 points by converting winger Sam Perrett's third try in the 64th minute after beginning the match four points behind the Canberra forward. In August 2008, Fitzgibbon was named in the [[Australia national rugby league team#Rugby League World Cup Squad|preliminary 46-man Kangaroos squad]] for the [[2008 Rugby League World Cup]],<ref>{{cite news|author=Liam FitzGibbon|publisher=Fox Sports News (Australia)|title=Surprises in Kangaroos squad|url=http://www.foxsports.com.au/story/0,8659,24111780-23214,00.html|access-date=2 August 2008|date=1 August 2008|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080808144730/http://www.foxsports.com.au/story/0%2C8659%2C24111780-23214%2C00.html|archive-date=8 August 2008|df=dmy-all}}</ref><ref name="Veteran Lockyer named in Australian squad">{{cite web|url=http://www.iht.com/articles/ap/2008/08/01/sports/AS-RGL-Australia-Squad.php|title=Veteran Lockyer named in Australian squad|publisher=International Herald Tribune|date=1 August 2008|access-date=2 August 2008}}</ref> and in October 2008 he was selected in the final 24-man [[2008 Rugby League World Cup squads#Australia|Australia]] squad.<ref name="Manly six win Australia call-up">{{cite web|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/7656211.stm|title=Manly six win Australia call-up|publisher=BBC|date=7 October 2008|access-date=7 October 2008}}</ref> On 16 May 2009 it was announced that Craig had signed a one-year deal, with the option of a second year, with [[Super League]] side [[Hull F.C.]] for the 2010 season.<ref>[https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/super_league/hull/8053856.stm Fitzgibbon agrees to join Hull FC]</ref>
[[File:Fitz kick2 zoom.jpg|thumb|right|Craig Fitzgibbon lining up a conversion in 2009]]

===Super League===
Craig Fitzgibbon was selected for the [[Exiles rugby league team|Exiles]] squad for the [[International Origin]] match against [[England national rugby league team|England]] at [[Headingley Rugby Stadium|Headingley]] on 10 June 2011.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/13278410|title=Brian McClennan completes Exiles squad to face England fixture|work=BBC Sport|date=4 May 2011|archive-date=13 January 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160113141331/https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/13278410|url-status=live}}</ref> On 3 September 2011 Fitzgibbon suffered a fracture and ligament damage to his ankle and announced his retirement at the age of 34. On the 26 September announced he would return to the [[Sydney Roosters]] in 2012 as a part of its Coaching Staff.<ref>[https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/14827593 Hull FC's Craig Fitzgibbon retires early with injury]</ref>

===Highlights===
* Played in the 1999, 2000, 2002, 2003 & 2004 Grand Finals (scored 3 tries & kicked 9 goals for 30 points)
* Won the [[Clive Churchill Medal]] in the 2002 Grand Final
* Played 8 games for New South Wales 2003–2005 (scored one try & kicked 16 goals for 36 points)
* Played 15 games for Australia 2002–2005 (scored 3 tries & kicked 39 goals for 90 points)
* Played 4 games for Country vs. City 2002, 2004, 2005 & 2006
* Craig Fitzgibbon is the highest point scoring [[rugby league positions|forward]] (1560 points)
* Has played 239 first grade games (scored 39 tries & kicked 700 goals for 1560 Points)
* Has kicked over 600 goals for the Sydney Roosters
* One of the last remaining [[Illawarra Steelers]] players (Along with [[Trent Barrett]] and [[Luke Patten]])

==Coaching career==
On 24 August 2015, Fitzgibbon was named as coach for the [[Country New South Wales rugby league team]], replacing [[Trent Barrett]] for 2016.<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://crlnsw.com.au/fitzgibbon-to-coach-country/ |title=Fitzgibbon to Coach Country |date=24 August 2015 |access-date=18 January 2016 |archive-date=4 March 2016 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304194026/http://crlnsw.com.au/fitzgibbon-to-coach-country/ |url-status=dead }}</ref>
Fitzgibbon announced in April 2021 that he had signed a three-year contract to coach the [[Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks]] from 2022 onwards. Fitzgibbon was forced to miss coaching the Sharks' first game of the [[2022 NRL season]] as he was in isolation due to contracting [[COVID-19]].<ref>{{cite web |last1=Pengilly |first1=Adam |title=Sharks coach Fitzgibbon to miss first NRL coaching game due to COVID-19 |url=https://www.smh.com.au/sport/nrl/sharks-coach-fitzgibbon-to-miss-first-nrl-coaching-game-due-to-covid-19-20220309-p5a35p.html |website=smh.com.au |publisher=Nine Media |access-date=17 March 2022 |date=9 March 2022}}</ref> Assistant coach [[Steve Price (rugby league, born 1977)|Steve Price]] deputized for him, with Fitzgibbon working remotely.  In Fitzgibbon's first year as Cronulla head coach, he guided the club to a second place finish on the table which qualified the club for the finals.  However, Cronulla would exit the finals series in disappointing fashion, going out in straight sets after losing to [[North Queensland Cowboys|North Queensland]] in extra-time and then to [[South Sydney Rabbitohs|South Sydney]] the following week.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.foxsports.com.au/nrl/nrl-premiership/cronullas-dual-gamble-pays-off-major-flaw-exposed-in-finals-disaster-brutal-review/news-story/619a3957ee64720ef66e0e9542f375b7/|title=Young gun who can solve Sharks' big problem; masterstroke that can't be overlooked: Brutal Review|website=www.foxsports.com.au|date=19 September 2022 }}</ref>

On 14 February 2023, Fitzgibbon signed a five-year contract extension to remain as Cronulla's head coach until the end of the 2027 season.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.foxsports.com.au/nrl/nrl-premiership/nrl-2023-craig-fitzgibbon-cronulla-sharks-resigning-extension-2027-new-contract-new-deal-how-long-is-he-signed-for/news-story/ad799936db50d5b0647057d57869cab9/|title=Sharks lock down coach Fitzgibbon on FIVE-YEAR deal following stellar rookie season|website=www.foxsports.com.au|date=14 February 2023 }}</ref>
Fitzgibbon guided Cronulla to a sixth place finish in the [[2023 NRL season]].  Cronulla would go on to be eliminated in the first week of the finals being upset by the Sydney Roosters 13-12.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.abc.net.au/news/2023-09-09/nrl-finals-panthers-warriors-sharks-roosters-live-updates/102816232/|title=Sydney Roosters score 'wild' 13-12 NRL elimination final win over Cronulla Sharks, Penrith Panthers beat Warriors|newspaper=ABC News|date=9 September 2023 }}</ref>
Fitzgibbon would lead Cronulla to a 4th place finish in the [[2024 NRL season]]. Cronulla would lose the Qualifying Final against the [[Melbourne Storm]] 37-10.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.smh.com.au/sport/nrl/nrl-finals-2024-qualifying-final-live-updates-melbourne-storm-cronulla-sharks-chase-finals-glory-at-aami-park-20240913-p5kah5.html | title=Grant's hat-trick leads Storm to qualifying final win over Sharks | date=14 September 2024 }}</ref> They would later win their semi final match against the [[North Queensland Cowboys]] 26-18.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.smh.com.au/sport/nrl/nrl-finals-live-cronulla-sutherland-sharks-v-north-queensland-cowboys-20240920-p5kca6.html | title=NRL finals as it happened: Sharks snap finals losing streak with victory over the Cowboys | date=20 September 2024 }}</ref> Cronulla would progress to the preliminary finals before losing to the [[Penrith Panthers]] 26-6.<ref>{{cite news | url=https://www.abc.net.au/news/2024-09-28/nrl-live-penrith-panthers-cronulla-sharks-preliminary-final/104400568 | title=Nathan Cleary shrugs off late injury scare as Panthers reach fifth straight NRL grand final | newspaper=ABC News | date=28 September 2024 }}</ref>
In the [[2025 NRL season]], Fitzgibbon guided Cronulla to 5th place on the table. Cronulla would then defeat the Sydney Roosters in week one of the finals before defeating the minor premiers Canberra to set up a preliminary final match against Melbourne. Cronulla would lose the game 22-14 falling short of the grand final for a second consecutive season.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.nine.com.au/sport/nrl/the-mole-2025-season-review-cronulla-sharks-signings-2026-20250930-p5myv7.html/|title=The Mole's season review: Brutal reality facing Sharks as 'marquee playmaker' looms as only saviour|website=www.nine.com.au |date=29 September 2025 }}</ref>
